meta.title: Characterization of genotype-specific epitopes of the HN protein of mumps virus

abstractHtml: The SBL-1 strain of mumps virus, grouping with genotype A on the basis of the small hydrophobic protein gene sequence, was grown in the presence of three different monoclonal antibodies. The monoclonal antibodies were directed against the haemagglutinin-neuraminidase (HN) protein and they inhibited haemagglutinating activity and infectivity of the virus. The HN genes of the nine neutralization-escape virus mutants were sequenced and the predicted amino acid sequences were compared with that of the parental virus. Amino acid substitutions were found at positions 269, 352 and 354, respectively, of the 582 amino acid long protein. The three monoclonal antibodies did not react with 35 virus strains isolated in Stockholm during the years 1970 to 1985. Thirteen and four of the strains were found to belong to the D and C genotypes, respectively. A type-specific neutralization antibody response was also found in sera of rabbits hyperimmunized with purified virions of genotype A and D. The genotype-specific difference in neutralizing activity in mice and rabbits was not corroborated by an overall difference in the amino acid sequence of the HN protein of the different genotypes. Further studies are needed to explore the efficacy of mumps virus vaccines for protection against homologous and heterologous genotypes of mumps virus.
summaryHtml:
Researchers characterized genotype-specific epitopes on the hemagglutinin-neuraminidase (HN) protein of mumps virus by growing the SBL-1 strain (genotype A) with three neutralizing monoclonal antibodies and sequencing escape mutants. Nine virus mutants were generated, revealing amino acid substitutions at positions 269, 352, and 354 of the 582-amino acid HN protein. The three antibodies that blocked hemagglutinating activity did not react with naturally circulating genotype C or D strains, correlating with sequence differences at positions 265-266 and 354-356. In contrast, antibodies blocking neuraminidase activity cross-reacted with all genotypes tested. Rabbit hyperimmune sera directed against genotype A or D showed type-specific neutralizing antibody responses, with type-specific antibodies dominating over cross-reactive ones. These findings indicate that neutralizing antibodies preferentially target epitopes where amino acid variation occurs between mumps virus genotypes. The results raise concerns about vaccine efficacy, suggesting that mumps vaccines containing a single genotype may provide suboptimal protection against heterologous genotypes circulating in nature.

Key findings

  • Three monoclonal antibodies that inhibit hemagglutination of genotype A mumps virus selected escape mutants with amino acid substitutions at HN protein positions 269, 352, and 354
  • These three antibodies did not react with naturally circulating genotype C and D mumps virus strains, which have different amino acids at positions 265-266 and 354-356
  • Rabbit hyperimmune sera showed type-specific neutralizing antibody responses dominated by antibodies targeting genotype-specific epitopes rather than cross-reactive epitopes
  • Antibodies blocking neuraminidase activity cross-reacted with all tested genotypes, suggesting different epitope locations compared to hemagglutination-blocking antibodies
  • The findings suggest that single-genotype mumps vaccines may provide incomplete protection against heterologous mumps virus genotypes
